_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

## Summary

Successfully implemented the combined approach for validating `FakeKafkaBroker` against the real `FastStream KafkaBroker`:

### What was done

1. **Updated `FakeKafkaBroker`** in `conftest.py` to manually match all parameters from the real `KafkaBroker.publish()` method
2. **Added type safety** by importing the real `KafkaBroker` class and using it in a helper function that Mypy can validate
3. **Created runtime contract test** (`test_fake_broker_contract.py`) that uses `inspect.signature()` to automatically verify parameter names, defaults, and type annotations

### Key discoveries

**The runtime inspection test caught real bugs!**

During implementation, it automatically detected:
- Missing parameter: `no_confirm` (we had no idea this existed)
- Wrong default values: `correlation_id` was `""` instead of `None`
- Several other subtle mismatches in type annotations and defaults

**This proves the value of runtime inspection** - even with careful manual work, we missed critical details that the automated test caught immediately.

### Benefits of combined approach

- **Mypy (static)**: Catches type errors during development
- **pytest (runtime)**: Validates signature compatibility without needing Kafka running
- **No infrastructure**: Works with class definitions only
- **Future-proof**: Automatically detects when FastStream updates their API

All tests pass, Mypy is happy, and we now have robust protection against contract drift. Documentation saved to `docs/testing/test-double-contract-validation.md`.

---

Also need add comments for Contract tests / inspection tests

This does not have to be the full node list. It just needs to have at least one broker that will respond to a Metadata API Request. Default port is 9092.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Union[str, Iterable[str]]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;'localhost'&#96;\n\nClient request timeout in milliseconds.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;40 * 1000&#96;\n\nMilliseconds to backoff when retrying on errors.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;100&#96;\n\nThe period of time in milliseconds after which we force a refresh of metadata even if we haven't seen any partition leadership changes to proactively discover any new brokers or partitions.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;5 * 60 * 1000&#96;\n\nClose idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this config. Specifying&#96;None&#96; will disable idle checks.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;9 * 60 * 1000&#96;\n\nKerberos service name.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;'kafka'&#96;\n\nKerberos domain name.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[str]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nOAuthBearer token provider instance.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[AbstractTokenProvider]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nEvent loop to use.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[AbstractEventLoop]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nA name for this client. This string is passed in each request to servers and can be used to identify specific server-side log entries that correspond to this client. Also submitted to :class:&#96;~.consumer.group_coordinator.GroupCoordinator&#96; for logging with respect to consumer group administration.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[str]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;SERVICE_NAME&#96;\n\nOne of&#96;0&#96;,&#96;1&#96;,&#96;all&#96;. The number of acknowledgments the producer requires the leader to have received before considering a request complete. This controls the durability of records that are sent. The following settings are common: *&#96;0&#96;: Producer will not wait for any acknowledgment from the server at all. The message will immediately be added to the socket buffer and considered sent. No guarantee can be made that the server has received the record in this case, and the retries configuration will not take effect (as the client won't generally know of any failures). The offset given back for each record will always be set to -1. *&#96;1&#96;: The broker leader will write the record to its local log but will respond without awaiting full acknowledgement from all followers. In this case should the leader fail immediately after acknowledging the record but before the followers have replicated it then the record will be lost. *&#96;all&#96;: The broker leader will wait for the full set of in-sync replicas to acknowledge the record. This guarantees that the record will not be lost as long as at least one in-sync replica remains alive. This is the strongest available guarantee. If unset, defaults to&#96;acks=1&#96;. If&#96;enable_idempotence&#96; is :data:&#96;True&#96; defaults to&#96;acks=all&#96;.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Union[Literal[0, 1, -1, 'all'], object]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;_missing&#96;\n\nUsed to convert user-supplied keys to bytes.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[Callable[[Any], bytes]]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nUsed to convert user-supplied message values to bytes.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[Callable[[Any], bytes]]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nThe compression type for all data generated by the producer. Compression is of full batches of data, so the efficacy of batching will also impact the compression ratio (more batching means better compression).\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[Literal['gzip', 'snappy', 'lz4', 'zstd']]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nMaximum size of buffered data per partition. After this amount&#96;send&#96; coroutine will block until batch is drained.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;16 * 1024&#96;\n\nCallable used to determine which partition each message is assigned to. Called (after key serialization):&#96;partitioner(key_bytes, all_partitions, available_partitions)&#96;. The default partitioner implementation hashes each non-None key using the same murmur2 algorithm as the Java client so that messages with the same key are assigned to the same partition. When a key is :data:&#96;None&#96;, the message is delivered to a random partition (filtered to partitions with available leaders only, if possible).\n\nTYPE:&#96;Callable&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;DefaultPartitioner()&#96;\n\nThe maximum size of a request. This is also effectively a cap on the maximum record size. Note that the server has its own cap on record size which may be different from this. This setting will limit the number of record batches the producer will send in a single request to avoid sending huge requests.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;1024 * 1024&#96;\n\nThe producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they can be sent out. However in some circumstances the client may want to reduce the number of requests even under moderate load. This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount of artificial delay; that is, if first request is processed faster, than&#96;linger_ms&#96;, producer will wait&#96;linger_ms - process_time&#96;.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;0&#96;\n\nWhen set to&#96;True&#96;, the producer will ensure that exactly one copy of each message is written in the stream. If&#96;False&#96;, producer retries due to broker failures, etc., may write duplicates of the retried message in the stream. Note that enabling idempotence acks to set to&#96;all&#96;. If it is not explicitly set by the user it will be chosen.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;False&#96;\n\nTransactional id for the producer.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[str]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nTransaction timeout in milliseconds.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;60 * 1000&#96;\n\nGraceful shutdown timeout. Broker waits for all running subscribers completion before shut down.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[float]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;15.0&#96;\n\nCustom decoder object.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[CustomCallable]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nCustom parser object.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[CustomCallable]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nDependencies to apply to all broker subscribers.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Iterable[Dependant]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nMiddlewares to apply to all broker publishers/subscribers.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Sequence[BrokerMiddlewarep[Any, Any]]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nRouters to apply to broker.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Sequence[Registrator]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nSecurity options to connect broker and generate AsyncAPI server security information.\n\nTYPE: [BaseSecurity](https://faststream.ag2.ai/latest/api/faststream/security/BaseSecurity/#faststream.security.BaseSecurity)&#96;Optional[]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nAsyncAPI hardcoded server addresses. Use&#96;servers&#96; if not specified.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Union[str, Iterable[str], None]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nAsyncAPI server protocol.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[str]&#96; Literal[\"gzip\", \"snappy\", \"lz4\", \"zstd\"] | None = None,\n    max_batch_size: int = 16 * 1024,\n    partitioner: Callable[\n        [bytes, list[Partition], list[Partition]],\n        Partition,\n    ] = DefaultPartitioner(),  # noqa: B008\n    max_request_size: int = 1024 * 1024,\n    linger_ms: int = 0,\n    enable_idempotence: bool = False,\n    transactional_id: str | None = None,\n    transaction_timeout_ms: int = 60 * 1000,\n    # broker base args\n    graceful_timeout: float | None = 15.0,\n    decoder: Optional[\"CustomCallable\"] = None,\n    parser: Optional[\"CustomCallable\"] = None,\n    dependencies: Iterable[\"Dependant\"] = (),\n    middlewares: Sequence[\"BrokerMiddleware[Any, Any]\"] = (),\n    routers: Iterable[KafkaRegistrator] = (),\n None,\n    description: str | None = None,\n    tags: Iterable[Union[\"Tag\", \"TagDict\"]] = (),\n    # logging args\n    logger: Optional[\"LoggerProto\"] = EMPTY,\n    log_level: int = logging.INFO,\n    # FastDepends args\n    apply_types: bool = True,\n    serializer: Optional[\"SerializerProto\"] = EMPTY,\n    provider: Optional[\"Provider\"] = None,\n    context: Optional[\"ContextRepo\"] = None,\n) -\u003e None:\n    \"\"\"Kafka broker constructor.\n\n    Args:\n        bootstrap_servers (Union[str, Iterable[str]]):\n            A &#96;host[:port]&#96; string (or list of &#96;host[:port]&#96; strings) that the consumer should contact to bootstrap\n            initial cluster metadata. This does not have to be the full node list.\n            It just needs to have at least one broker that will respond to a\n            Metadata API Request. Default port is 9092.\n        request_timeout_ms (int):\n            Client request timeout in milliseconds.\n        retry_backoff_ms (int):\n            Milliseconds to backoff when retrying on errors.\n        metadata_max_age_ms (int):\n            The period of time in milliseconds after which we force a refresh of metadata even if we haven't seen any\n            partition leadership changes to proactively discover any new brokers or partitions.\n        connections_max_idle_ms (int):\n            Close idle connections after the number of milliseconds specified by this config. Specifying &#96;None&#96; will\n            disable idle checks.\n        sasl_kerberos_service_name (str):\n            Kerberos service name.\n        sasl_kerberos_domain_name (Optional[str]):\n            Kerberos domain name.\n        sasl_oauth_token_provider (Optional[AbstractTokenProvider]):\n            OAuthBearer token provider instance.\n        loop (Optional[asyncio.AbstractEventLoop]):\n            Event loop to use.\n        client_id (Optional[str]):\n            A name for this client. This string is passed in each request to servers and can be used to identify specific\n            server-side log entries that correspond to this client. Also submitted to :class:&#96;~.consumer.group_coordinator.GroupCoordinator&#96;\n            for logging with respect to consumer group administration.\n        acks (Union[Literal[0, 1, -1, \"all\"], object]):\n            One of &#96;&#96;0&#96;&#96;, &#96;&#96;1&#96;&#96;, &#96;&#96;all&#96;&#96;. The number of acknowledgments the producer requires the leader to have received before considering a\n            request complete. This controls the durability of records that are sent. The following settings are common:\n            * &#96;&#96;0&#96;&#96;: Producer will not wait for any acknowledgment from the server at all. The message will immediately be added to the socket\n              buffer and considered sent. No guarantee can be made that the server has received the record in this case, and the retries\n              configuration will not take effect (as the client won't generally know of any failures). The offset given back for each\n              record will always be set to -1.\n            * &#96;&#96;1&#96;&#96;: The broker leader will write the record to its local log but will respond without awaiting full acknowledgement from all\n              followers. In this case should the leader fail immediately after acknowledging the record but before the followers have\n              replicated it then the record will be lost.\n            * &#96;&#96;all&#96;&#96;: The broker leader will wait for the full set of in-sync replicas to acknowledge the record. This guarantees that the\n              record will not be lost as long as at least one in-sync replica remains alive. This is the strongest available guarantee.\n            If unset, defaults to &#96;&#96;acks=1&#96;&#96;. If &#96;enable_idempotence&#96; is :data:&#96;True&#96; defaults to &#96;&#96;acks=all&#96;&#96;.\n        key_serializer (Optional[Callable[[Any], bytes]]):\n            Used to convert user-supplied keys to bytes.\n        value_serializer (Optional[Callable[[Any], bytes]]):\n            Used to convert user-supplied message values to bytes.\n        compression_type (Optional[Literal[\"gzip\", \"snappy\", \"lz4\", \"zstd\"]]):\n            The compression type for all data generated by the producer.\n            Compression is of full batches of data, so the efficacy of batching will also impact the compression ratio (more batching means better compression).\n        max_batch_size (int):\n            Maximum size of buffered data per partition. After this amount &#96;send&#96; coroutine will block until batch is drained.\n        partitioner (Callable):\n            Callable used to determine which partition each message is assigned to. Called (after key serialization):\n            &#96;&#96;partitioner(key_bytes, all_partitions, available_partitions)&#96;&#96;.\n            The default partitioner implementation hashes each non-None key using the same murmur2 algorithm as the Java client so that\n            messages with the same key are assigned to the same partition. When a key is :data:&#96;None&#96;, the message is delivered to a random partition\n            (filtered to partitions with available leaders only, if possible).\n        max_request_size (int):\n            The maximum size of a request. This is also effectively a cap on the maximum record size. Note that the server\n            has its own cap on record size which may be different from this. This setting will limit the number of record batches the producer\n            will send in a single request to avoid sending huge requests.\n        linger_ms (int):\n            The producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request.\n            Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they can be sent out. However in some circumstances the client\n            may want to reduce the number of requests even under moderate load. This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount of\n            artificial delay; that is, if first request is processed faster, than &#96;linger_ms&#96;, producer will wait &#96;&#96;linger_ms - process_time&#96;&#96;.\n        enable_idempotence (bool):\n            When set to &#96;True&#96;, the producer will ensure that exactly one copy of each message is written in the stream.\n            If &#96;False&#96;, producer retries due to broker failures, etc., may write duplicates of the retried message in the stream.\n            Note that enabling idempotence acks to set to &#96;&#96;all&#96;&#96;. If it is not explicitly set by the user it will be chosen.\n        transactional_id (Optional[str]):\n            Transactional id for the producer.\n        transaction_timeout_ms (int):\n            Transaction timeout in milliseconds.\n        graceful_timeout (Optional[float]):\n            Graceful shutdown timeout. Broker waits for all running subscribers completion before shut down.\n        decoder (Optional[CustomCallable]):\n            Custom decoder object.\n        parser (Optional[CustomCallable]):\n            Custom parser object.\n        dependencies (Iterable[Dependant]):\n            Dependencies to apply to all broker subscribers.\n        middlewares (Sequence[BrokerMiddlewarep[Any, Any]]):\n            Middlewares to apply to all broker publishers/subscribers.\n        routers (Sequence[Registrator]):\n            Routers to apply to broker.\n        security (Optional[BaseSecurity]):\n            Security options to connect broker and generate AsyncAPI server security information.\n        specification_url (Union[str, Iterable[str], None]):\n            AsyncAPI hardcoded server addresses. Use &#96;servers&#96; if not specified.\n        protocol (Optional[str]):\n            AsyncAPI server protocol.\n        protocol_version (Optional[str]):\n            AsyncAPI server protocol version.\n        description (Optional[str]):\n            AsyncAPI server description.\n        tags (Iterable[Union[Tag, TagDict]]):\n            AsyncAPI server tags.\n        logger (Optional[LoggerProto]):\n            User specified logger to pass into Context and log service messages.\n        log_level (int):\n            Service messages log level.\n        apply_types (bool):\n            Whether to use FastDepends or not.\n        serializer (Optional[SerializerProto]):\n            Serializer to use.\n        provider (Optional[Provider]):\n            Provider for FastDepends.\n        context (Optional[ContextRepo]):\n            Context for FastDepends.\n    \"\"\"\n    if protocol is None:\n        if security is not None and security.use_ssl:\n            protocol = \"kafka-secure\"\n        else:\n            protocol = \"kafka\"\n\n    servers = (\n        [bootstrap_servers]\n        if isinstance(bootstrap_servers, str)\n        else list(bootstrap_servers)\n    )\n\n    if specification_url is not None:\n        if isinstance(specification_url, str):\n            specification_url = [specification_url]\n        else:\n            specification_url = list(specification_url)\n    else:\n        specification_url = servers\n\n    connection_params = dict(\n        bootstrap_servers=servers,\n        # both args\n        client_id=client_id,\n        request_timeout_ms=request_timeout_ms,\n        retry_backoff_ms=retry_backoff_ms,\n        metadata_max_age_ms=metadata_max_age_ms,\n        connections_max_idle_ms=connections_max_idle_ms,\n        sasl_kerberos_service_name=sasl_kerberos_service_name,\n        sasl_kerberos_domain_name=sasl_kerberos_domain_name,\n        sasl_oauth_token_provider=sasl_oauth_token_provider,\n        loop=loop,\n        # publisher args\n        acks=acks,\n        key_serializer=key_serializer,\n        value_serializer=value_serializer,\n        compression_type=compression_type,\n        max_batch_size=max_batch_size,\n        partitioner=partitioner,\n        max_request_size=max_request_size,\n        linger_ms=linger_ms,\n        enable_idempotence=enable_idempotence,\n        transactional_id=transactional_id,\n        transaction_timeout_ms=transaction_timeout_ms,\n        **parse_security(security),\n    )\n\n    if protocol_version:\n        connection_params[\"api_version\"] = protocol_version\n\n    consumer_options, _ = filter_by_dict(\n        ConsumerConnectionParams,\n        connection_params,\n    )\n    builder = partial(aiokafka.AIOKafkaConsumer, **consumer_options)\n\n    super().__init__(\n        **connection_params,\n        routers=routers,\n        config=KafkaBrokerConfig(\n            client_id=client_id,\n            builder=builder,\n            producer=AioKafkaFastProducerImpl(\n                parser=parser,\n                decoder=decoder,\n            ),\n            # both args,\n            broker_decoder=decoder,\n            broker_parser=parser,\n            broker_middlewares=middlewares,\n            logger=make_kafka_logger_state(\n                logger=logger,\n                log_level=log_level,\n            ),\n            fd_config=FastDependsConfig(\n                use_fastdepends=apply_types,\n                serializer=serializer,\n                provider=provider or dependency_provider,\n                context=context or ContextRepo(),\n            ),\n            # subscriber args\n            graceful_timeout=graceful_timeout,\n            broker_dependencies=dependencies,\n            extra_context={\n                \"broker\": self,\n            },\n        ),\n        specification=BrokerSpec(\n            description=description,\n            url=specification_url,\n            protocol=protocol,\n            protocol_version=protocol_version or \"auto\",\n            security=security,\n            tags=tags,\n        ),\n    )\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### middlewares property Literal[\n        \"read_uncommitted\", \"read_committed\"\n    ] = \"read_uncommitted\",\n    batch_timeout_ms: int = 200,\n    max_records: int | None = None,\n    listener: Optional[ConsumerRebalanceListener] = None,\n    pattern: str | None = None,\n    partitions: Collection[TopicPartition] = (),\n    persistent: bool = True,\n    dependencies: Iterable[Dependant] = (),\n    parser: Optional[CustomCallable] = None,\n    decoder: Optional[CustomCallable] = None,\n    middlewares: Sequence[SubscriberMiddleware[Any]] = (),\n    no_ack: bool = EMPTY,\n    max_workers: int | None = None,\n    ack_policy: AckPolicy = EMPTY,\n    no_reply: bool = False,\n    title: str | None = None,\n    description: str | None = None,\n    include_in_schema: bool = True,\n) -\u003e Union[\n    DefaultSubscriber,\n    BatchSubscriber,\n    ConcurrentDefaultSubscriber,\n    ConcurrentBetweenPartitionsSubscriber,\n]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nCreate a subscriber for Kafka topics.\n\nKafka topics to consume messages from.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nWhether to consume messages in batches or not.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;False&#96;\n\nName of the consumer group to join for dynamic partition assignment (if enabled), and to use for fetching and committing offsets. If&#96;None&#96;, auto-partition assignment (via group coordinator) and offset commits are disabled.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nName of the group instance ID used for static membership (KIP-345). If set, the consumer is treated as a static member, which means it does not join/leave the group on each restart, avoiding unnecessary rebalances.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nAny callable that takes a raw message&#96;bytes&#96; key and returns a deserialized one.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Callable[[bytes], Any] | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nAny callable that takes a raw message&#96;bytes&#96; value and returns a deserialized value.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Callable[[bytes], Any] | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nThe maximum amount of data the server should return for a fetch request. This is not an absolute maximum, if the first message in the first non-empty partition of the fetch is larger than this value, the message will still be returned to ensure that the consumer can make progress. NOTE: consumer performs fetches to multiple brokers in parallel so memory usage will depend on the number of brokers containing partitions for the topic.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;50 * 1024 * 1024&#96;\n\nMinimum amount of data the server should return for a fetch request, otherwise wait up to&#96;fetch_max_wait_ms&#96; for more data to accumulate.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;1&#96;\n\nThe maximum amount of time in milliseconds the server will block before answering the fetch request if there isn't sufficient data to immediately satisfy the requirement given by&#96;fetch_min_bytes&#96;.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;500&#96;\n\nThe maximum amount of data per-partition the server will return. The maximum total memory used for a request&#96;= #partitions * max_partition_fetch_bytes&#96;. This size must be at least as large as the maximum message size the server allows or else it is possible for the producer to send messages larger than the consumer can fetch. If that happens, the consumer can get stuck trying to fetch a large message on a certain partition.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;1 * 1024 * 1024&#96;\n\nA policy for resetting offsets on&#96;OffsetOutOfRangeError&#96; errors:\n\n- &#96;earliest&#96; will move to the oldest available message\n- &#96;latest&#96; will move to the most recent\n- &#96;none&#96; will raise an exception so you can handle this case\n\nTYPE:&#96;Literal['latest', 'earliest', 'none']&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;'latest'&#96;\n\nIf&#96;True&#96; the consumer's offset will be periodically committed in the background.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;EMPTY&#96;\n\nMilliseconds between automatic offset commits, if&#96;auto_commit&#96; is&#96;True&#96;.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;5 * 1000&#96;\n\nAutomatically check the CRC32 of the records consumed. This ensures no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so it may be disabled in cases seeking extreme performance.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;True&#96;\n\nList of objects to use to distribute partition ownership amongst consumer instances when group management is used. This preference is implicit in the order of the strategies in the list. When assignment strategy changes: to support a change to the assignment strategy, new versions must enable support both for the old assignment strategy and the new one. The coordinator will choose the old assignment strategy until all members have been updated. Then it will choose the new strategy.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Sequence[AbstractPartitionAssignor]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;(RoundRobinPartitionAssignor,)&#96;\n\nMaximum allowed time between calls to consume messages in batches. If this interval is exceeded the consumer is considered failed and the group will rebalance in order to reassign the partitions to another consumer group member. If API methods block waiting for messages, that time does not count against this timeout.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;5 * 60 * 1000&#96;\n\nThe maximum time server will wait for this consumer to rejoin the group in a case of rebalance. In Java client this behaviour is bound to&#96;max.poll.interval.ms&#96; configuration, but as&#96;aiokafka&#96; will rejoin the group in the background, we decouple this setting to allow finer tuning by users that use&#96;ConsumerRebalanceListener&#96; to delay rebalacing. Defaults to&#96;session_timeout_ms&#96;\n\nTYPE:&#96;int | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nClient group session and failure detection timeout. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats (&#96;heartbeat.interval.ms&#96;) to indicate its liveness to the broker. If no hearts are received by the broker for a group member within the session timeout, the broker will remove the consumer from the group and trigger a rebalance. The allowed range is configured with the broker configuration properties&#96;group.min.session.timeout.ms&#96; and&#96;group.max.session.timeout.ms&#96;.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;10 * 1000&#96;\n\nThe expected time in milliseconds between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using Kafka's group management feature. Heartbeats are used to ensure that the consumer's session stays active and to facilitate rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The value must be set lower than&#96;session_timeout_ms&#96;, but typically should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal rebalances.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;3 * 1000&#96;\n\nMaximum wait timeout for background fetching routine. Mostly defines how fast the system will see rebalance and request new data for new partitions.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;200&#96;\n\nThe maximum number of records returned in a single call by batch consumer. Has no limit by default.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nWhether records from internal topics (such as offsets) should be exposed to the consumer. If set to True the only way to receive records from an internal topic is subscribing to it.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;True&#96;\n\nControls how to read messages written transactionally.\n\n&#96;read_committed&#96;, batch consumer will only return transactional messages which have been committed.\n\n&#96;read_uncommitted&#96;(the default), batch consumer will return all messages, even transactional messages which have been aborted.\n\nNon-transactional messages will be returned unconditionally in either mode.\n\nMessages will always be returned in offset order. Hence, in&#96;read_committed&#96; mode, batch consumer will only return messages up to the last stable offset (ALSO), which is the one less than the offset of the first open transaction. In particular any messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions will be withheld until the relevant transaction has been completed. As a result,&#96;read_committed&#96; consumers will not be able to read up to the high watermark when there are in flight transactions. Further, when in&#96;read_committed&#96; the seek_to_end method will return the ALSO. See method docs below.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Literal['read_uncommitted', 'read_committed']&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;'read_uncommitted'&#96;\n\nMilliseconds spent waiting if data is not available in the buffer. If 0, returns immediately with any records that are available currently in the buffer, else returns empty.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;200&#96;\n\nNumber of messages to consume as one batch.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nOptionally include listener callback, which will be called before and after each rebalance operation. As part of group management, the consumer will keep track of the list of consumers that belong to a particular group and will trigger a rebalance operation if one of the following events trigger:\n\n- Number of partitions change for any of the subscribed topics\n- Topic is created or deleted\n- An existing member of the consumer group dies\n- A new member is added to the consumer group\n\nWhen any of these events are triggered, the provided listener will be invoked first to indicate that the consumer's assignment has been revoked, and then again when the new assignment has been received. Note that this listener will immediately override any listener set in a previous call to subscribe. It is guaranteed, however, that the partitions revoked/assigned through this interface are from topics subscribed in this call.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[ConsumerRebalanceListener]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nPattern to match available topics. You must provide either topics or pattern, but not both.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nAn explicit partitions list to assign. You can't use 'topics' and 'partitions' in the same time.\n\nTYPE: [TopicPartition](https://faststream.ag2.ai/latest/api/faststream/kafka/TopicPartition/#aiokafka.structs.TopicPartition)&#96;Collection[]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nDependencies list (&#96;[Dependant(),]&#96;) to apply to the subscriber.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Iterable[Dependant]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nParser to map original ConsumerRecord object to FastStream one.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[CustomCallable]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nFunction to decode FastStream msg bytes body to python objects.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Optional[CustomCallable]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nSubscriber middlewares to wrap incoming message processing.\n\nTYPE:&#96;Sequence[SubscriberMiddleware[Any]]&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;()&#96;\n\nNumber of workers to process messages concurrently.\n\nTYPE:&#96;int | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nWhether to disable FastStream auto acknowledgement logic or not.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;EMPTY&#96;\n\nAcknowledgement policy for the subscriber.\n\nTYPE: [AckPolicy](https://faststream.ag2.ai/latest/api/faststream/middlewares/AckPolicy/#faststream.middlewares.AckPolicy) DEFAULT:&#96;EMPTY&#96;\n\nWhether to disable FastStream RPC and Reply To auto responses or not.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;False&#96;\n\nSpecification subscriber object title.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nSpecification subscriber object description. \" \"Uses decorated docstring as default.\n\nTYPE:&#96;str | None&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;None&#96;\n\nWhetever to include operation in Specification schema or not.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;True&#96;\n\nWhether to make the subscriber persistent or not.\n\nTYPE:&#96;bool&#96; DEFAULT:&#96;True&#96;\n\n| PARAMETER | DESCRIPTION |\n| --- | --- |\n| &#96;*topics&#96; |\n| &#96;batch&#96; |\n| &#96;group_id&#96; |\n| &#96;group_instance_id&#96; |\n| &#96;key_deserializer&#96; |\n| &#96;value_deserializer&#96; |\n| &#96;fetch_max_bytes&#96; |\n| &#96;fetch_min_bytes&#96; |\n| &#96;fetch_max_wait_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;max_partition_fetch_bytes&#96; |\n| &#96;auto_offset_reset&#96; |\n| &#96;auto_commit&#96; |\n| &#96;auto_commit_interval_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;check_crcs&#96; |\n| &#96;partition_assignment_strategy&#96; |\n| &#96;max_poll_interval_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;rebalance_timeout_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;session_timeout_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;heartbeat_interval_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;consumer_timeout_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;max_poll_records&#96; |\n| &#96;exclude_internal_topics&#96; |\n| &#96;isolation_level&#96; |\n| &#96;batch_timeout_ms&#96; |\n| &#96;max_records&#96; |\n| &#96;listener&#96; |\n| &#96;pattern&#96; |\n| &#96;partitions&#96; |\n| &#96;dependencies&#96; |\n| &#96;parser&#96; |\n| &#96;decoder&#96; |\n| &#96;middlewares&#96; |\n| &#96;max_workers&#96; |\n| &#96;no_ack&#96; |\n| &#96;ack_policy&#96; |\n| &#96;no_reply&#96; |\n| &#96;title&#96; |\n| &#96;description&#96; |\n| &#96;include_in_schema&#96; |\n| &#96;persistent&#96; |\n\nSource code in&#96;faststream/kafka/broker/registrator.py&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n407\n408\n409\n410\n411\n412\n413\n414\n415\n416\n417\n418\n419\n420\n421\n422\n423\n424\n425\n426\n427\n428\n429\n430\n431\n432\n433\n434\n435\n436\n437\n438\n439\n440\n441\n442\n443\n444\n445\n446\n447\n448\n449\n450\n451\n452\n453\n454\n455\n456\n457\n458\n459\n460\n461\n462\n463\n464\n465\n466\n467\n468\n469\n470\n471\n472\n473\n474\n475\n476\n477\n478\n479\n480\n481\n482\n483\n484\n485\n486\n487\n488\n489\n490\n491\n492\n493\n494\n495\n496\n497\n498\n499\n500\n501\n502\n503\n504\n505\n506\n507\n508\n509\n510\n511\n512\n513\n514\n515\n516\n517\n518\n519\n520\n521\n522\n523\n524\n525\n526\n527\n528\n529\n530\n531\n532\n533\n534\n535\n536\n537\n538\n539\n540\n541\n542\n543\n544\n545\n546\n547\n548\n549\n550\n551\n552\n553\n554\n555\n556\n557\n558\n559\n560\n561\n562\n563\n564\n565\n566\n567\n568\n569\n570\n571\n572\n573\n574\n575\n576\n577\n578\n579\n580\n581\n582\n583\n584\n585\n586\n587\n588\n589\n590\n591\n592\n593\n594\n595\n596\n597\n598\n599\n600\n601\n602\n603\n604\n605\n606\n607\n608\n609\n610\n611\n612\n613\n614\n615\n616\n617\n618\n619\n620\n621\n622\n623\n624\n625\n626\n627\n628\n629\n630\n631\n632\n633\n634\n635\n636\n637\n638\n639\n640\n641\n642\n643\n644\n645\n646\n647\n648\n649\n650\n651\n652\n653\n654\n655\n656\n657\n658\n659\n660\n661\n662\n663\n664\n665\n666\n667\n668\n669\n670\n671\n672\n673\n674\n675\n676\n677\n678\n679\n680\n681\n682\n683\n684\n685\n686\n687\n688\n689\n690\n691\n692\n693\n694\n695\n696\n697\n698\n699\n700\n701\n702\n703\n704\n705\n706\n707\n708\n709\n710\n711\n712\n713\n714\n715\n716\n717\n718\n719\n720\n721\n722\n723\n724\n725\n726\n727\n728\n729\n730\n731\n732\n733\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n@override\ndef subscriber(\n    self,\n    *topics: str,\n    batch: bool = False,\n    group_id: str | None = None,\n    group_instance_id: str | None = None,\n    key_deserializer: Callable[[bytes], Any] | None = None,\n    value_deserializer: Callable[[bytes], Any] | None = None,\n    fetch_max_bytes: int = 50 * 1024 * 1024,\n    fetch_min_bytes: int = 1,\n    fetch_max_wait_ms: int = 500,\n    max_partition_fetch_bytes: int = 1 * 1024 * 1024,\n    auto_offset_reset: Literal[\"latest\", \"earliest\", \"none\"] = \"latest\",\n    auto_commit: Annotated[\n        bool,\n        deprecated(\n            \"This option is deprecated and will be removed in 0.7.0 release. \"\n            \"Please, use &#96;ack_policy=AckPolicy.ACK_FIRST&#96; instead.\"\n        ),\n    ] = EMPTY,\n    auto_commit_interval_ms: int = 5 * 1000,\n    check_crcs: bool = True,\n    partition_assignment_strategy: Sequence[\"AbstractPartitionAssignor\"] = (\n        RoundRobinPartitionAssignor,\n    ),\n    max_poll_interval_ms: int = 5 * 60 * 1000,\n    rebalance_timeout_ms: int | None = None,\n    session_timeout_ms: int = 10 * 1000,\n    heartbeat_interval_ms: int = 3 * 1000,\n    consumer_timeout_ms: int = 200,\n    max_poll_records: int | None = None,\n    exclude_internal_topics: bool = True,\n    isolation_level: Literal[\n        \"read_uncommitted\", \"read_committed\"\n    ] = \"read_uncommitted\",\n    batch_timeout_ms: int = 200,\n    max_records: int | None = None,\n    listener: Optional[\"ConsumerRebalanceListener\"] = None,\n    pattern: str | None = None,\n    partitions: Collection[\"TopicPartition\"] = (),\n    # broker args\n    persistent: bool = True,\n    dependencies: Iterable[\"Dependant\"] = (),\n    parser: Optional[\"CustomCallable\"] = None,\n    decoder: Optional[\"CustomCallable\"] = None,\n    middlewares: Annotated[\n        Sequence[\"SubscriberMiddleware[Any]\"],\n        deprecated(\n            \"This option was deprecated in 0.6.0. Use router-level middlewares instead. \"\n            \"Scheduled to remove in 0.7.0\",\n        ),\n    ] = (),\n    no_ack: Annotated[\n        bool,\n        deprecated(\n            \"This option was deprecated in 0.6.0 to prior to **ack_policy=AckPolicy.MANUAL**. \"\n            \"Scheduled to remove in 0.7.0\",\n        ),\n    ] = EMPTY,\n    max_workers: int | None = None,\n    ack_policy: AckPolicy = EMPTY,\n    no_reply: bool = False,\n    # Specification args\n    title: str | None = None,\n    description: str | None = None,\n    include_in_schema: bool = True,\n) -\u003e Union[\n    \"DefaultSubscriber\",\n    \"BatchSubscriber\",\n    \"ConcurrentDefaultSubscriber\",\n    \"ConcurrentBetweenPartitionsSubscriber\",\n]:\n    \"\"\"Create a subscriber for Kafka topics.\n\n    Args:\n        *topics: Kafka topics to consume messages from.\n        batch: Whether to consume messages in batches or not.\n        group_id:\n            Name of the consumer group to join for dynamic\n            partition assignment (if enabled), and to use for fetching and\n            committing offsets. If &#96;None&#96;, auto-partition assignment (via\n            group coordinator) and offset commits are disabled.\n        group_instance_id:\n            Name of the group instance ID used for static\n            membership (KIP-345). If set, the consumer is treated as a\n            static member, which means it does not join/leave the group\n            on each restart, avoiding unnecessary rebalances.\n        key_deserializer:\n            Any callable that takes a raw message &#96;bytes&#96;\n            key and returns a deserialized one.\n        value_deserializer:\n            Any callable that takes a raw message &#96;bytes&#96;\n            value and returns a deserialized value.\n        fetch_max_bytes:\n            The maximum amount of data the server should\n            return for a fetch request. This is not an absolute maximum, if\n            the first message in the first non-empty partition of the fetch\n            is larger than this value, the message will still be returned\n            to ensure that the consumer can make progress. NOTE: consumer\n            performs fetches to multiple brokers in parallel so memory\n            usage will depend on the number of brokers containing\n            partitions for the topic.\n        fetch_min_bytes:\n            Minimum amount of data the server should\n            return for a fetch request, otherwise wait up to\n            &#96;fetch_max_wait_ms&#96; for more data to accumulate.\n        fetch_max_wait_ms:\n            The maximum amount of time in milliseconds\n            the server will block before answering the fetch request if\n            there isn't sufficient data to immediately satisfy the\n            requirement given by &#96;fetch_min_bytes&#96;.\n        max_partition_fetch_bytes:\n            The maximum amount of data\n            per-partition the server will return. The maximum total memory\n            used for a request &#96;&#96;= #partitions * max_partition_fetch_bytes&#96;&#96;.\n            This size must be at least as large as the maximum message size\n            the server allows or else it is possible for the producer to\n            send messages larger than the consumer can fetch. If that\n            happens, the consumer can get stuck trying to fetch a large\n            message on a certain partition.\n        auto_offset_reset:\n            A policy for resetting offsets on &#96;OffsetOutOfRangeError&#96; errors:\n\n            * &#96;earliest&#96; will move to the oldest available message\n            * &#96;latest&#96; will move to the most recent\n            * &#96;none&#96; will raise an exception so you can handle this case\n        auto_commit:\n            If &#96;True&#96; the consumer's offset will be\n            periodically committed in the background.\n\n        auto_commit_interval_ms:\n            Milliseconds between automatic\n            offset commits, if &#96;auto_commit&#96; is &#96;True&#96;.\n        check_crcs:\n            Automatically check the CRC32 of the records\n            consumed. This ensures no on-the-wire or on-disk corruption to\n            the messages occurred. This check adds some overhead, so it may\n            be disabled in cases seeking extreme performance.\n        partition_assignment_strategy:\n            List of objects to use to\n            distribute partition ownership amongst consumer instances when\n            group management is used. This preference is implicit in the order\n            of the strategies in the list. When assignment strategy changes:\n            to support a change to the assignment strategy, new versions must\n            enable support both for the old assignment strategy and the new\n            one. The coordinator will choose the old assignment strategy until\n            all members have been updated. Then it will choose the new\n            strategy.\n        max_poll_interval_ms:\n            Maximum allowed time between calls to\n            consume messages in batches. If this interval\n            is exceeded the consumer is considered failed and the group will\n            rebalance in order to reassign the partitions to another consumer\n            group member. If API methods block waiting for messages, that time\n            does not count against this timeout.\n        rebalance_timeout_ms:\n            The maximum time server will wait for this\n            consumer to rejoin the group in a case of rebalance. In Java client\n            this behaviour is bound to &#96;max.poll.interval.ms&#96; configuration,\n            but as &#96;&#96;aiokafka&#96;&#96; will rejoin the group in the background, we\n            decouple this setting to allow finer tuning by users that use\n            &#96;ConsumerRebalanceListener&#96; to delay rebalacing. Defaults\n            to &#96;&#96;session_timeout_ms&#96;&#96;\n        session_timeout_ms:\n            Client group session and failure detection\n            timeout. The consumer sends periodic heartbeats\n            (&#96;heartbeat.interval.ms&#96;) to indicate its liveness to the broker.\n            If no hearts are received by the broker for a group member within\n            the session timeout, the broker will remove the consumer from the\n            group and trigger a rebalance. The allowed range is configured with\n            the **broker** configuration properties\n            &#96;group.min.session.timeout.ms&#96; and &#96;group.max.session.timeout.ms&#96;.\n        heartbeat_interval_ms:\n            The expected time in milliseconds\n            between heartbeats to the consumer coordinator when using\n            Kafka's group management feature. Heartbeats are used to ensure\n            that the consumer's session stays active and to facilitate\n            rebalancing when new consumers join or leave the group. The\n            value must be set lower than &#96;session_timeout_ms&#96;, but typically\n            should be set no higher than 1/3 of that value. It can be\n            adjusted even lower to control the expected time for normal\n            rebalances.\n        consumer_timeout_ms:\n            Maximum wait timeout for background fetching\n            routine. Mostly defines how fast the system will see rebalance and\n            request new data for new partitions.\n        max_poll_records:\n            The maximum number of records returned in a\n            single call by batch consumer. Has no limit by default.\n        exclude_internal_topics:\n            Whether records from internal topics\n            (such as offsets) should be exposed to the consumer. If set to True\n            the only way to receive records from an internal topic is\n            subscribing to it.\n        isolation_level:\n            Controls how to read messages written\n            transactionally.\n\n            * &#96;read_committed&#96;, batch consumer will only return\n            transactional messages which have been committed.\n\n            * &#96;read_uncommitted&#96; (the default), batch consumer will\n            return all messages, even transactional messages which have been\n            aborted.\n\n            Non-transactional messages will be returned unconditionally in\n            either mode.\n\n            Messages will always be returned in offset order. Hence, in\n            &#96;read_committed&#96; mode, batch consumer will only return\n            messages up to the last stable offset (ALSO), which is the one less\n            than the offset of the first open transaction. In particular any\n            messages appearing after messages belonging to ongoing transactions\n            will be withheld until the relevant transaction has been completed.\n            As a result, &#96;read_committed&#96; consumers will not be able to read up\n            to the high watermark when there are in flight transactions.\n            Further, when in &#96;read_committed&#96; the seek_to_end method will\n            return the ALSO. See method docs below.\n        batch_timeout_ms:\n            Milliseconds spent waiting if\n            data is not available in the buffer. If 0, returns immediately\n            with any records that are available currently in the buffer,\n            else returns empty.\n        max_records: Number of messages to consume as one batch.\n        listener:\n            Optionally include listener\n            callback, which will be called before and after each rebalance\n            operation.\n            As part of group management, the consumer will keep track of\n            the list of consumers that belong to a particular group and\n            will trigger a rebalance operation if one of the following\n            events trigger:\n\n            * Number of partitions change for any of the subscribed topics\n            * Topic is created or deleted\n            * An existing member of the consumer group dies\n            * A new member is added to the consumer group\n\n            When any of these events are triggered, the provided listener\n            will be invoked first to indicate that the consumer's\n            assignment has been revoked, and then again when the new\n            assignment has been received. Note that this listener will\n            immediately override any listener set in a previous call\n            to subscribe. It is guaranteed, however, that the partitions\n            revoked/assigned\n            through this interface are from topics subscribed in this call.\n        pattern:\n            Pattern to match available topics. You must provide either topics or pattern, but not both.\n        partitions:  An explicit partitions list to assign.\n            You can't use 'topics' and 'partitions' in the same time.\n        dependencies: Dependencies list (&#96;[Dependant(),]&#96;) to apply to the subscriber.\n        parser: Parser to map original **ConsumerRecord** object to FastStream one.\n        decoder: Function to decode FastStream msg bytes body to python objects.\n        middlewares: Subscriber middlewares to wrap incoming message processing.\n        max_workers: Number of workers to process messages concurrently.\n        no_ack: Whether to disable **FastStream** auto acknowledgement logic or not.\n        ack_policy: Acknowledgement policy for the subscriber.\n        no_reply: Whether to disable **FastStream** RPC and Reply To auto responses or not.\n        title: Specification subscriber object title.\n        description: Specification subscriber object description. \" \"Uses decorated docstring as default.\n        include_in_schema: Whetever to include operation in Specification schema or not.\n        persistent: Whether to make the subscriber persistent or not.\n    \"\"\"\n    workers = max_workers or 1\n\n    subscriber = create_subscriber(\n        *topics,\n        batch=batch,\n        max_workers=workers,\n        batch_timeout_ms=batch_timeout_ms,\n        max_records=max_records,\n        group_id=group_id,\n        listener=listener,\n        pattern=pattern,\n        connection_args={\n            \"group_instance_id\": group_instance_id,\n            \"key_deserializer\": key_deserializer,\n            \"value_deserializer\": value_deserializer,\n            \"fetch_max_wait_ms\": fetch_max_wait_ms,\n            \"fetch_max_bytes\": fetch_max_bytes,\n            \"fetch_min_bytes\": fetch_min_bytes,\n            \"max_partition_fetch_bytes\": max_partition_fetch_bytes,\n            \"auto_offset_reset\": auto_offset_reset,\n            \"auto_commit_interval_ms\": auto_commit_interval_ms,\n            \"check_crcs\": check_crcs,\n            \"partition_assignment_strategy\": partition_assignment_strategy,\n            \"max_poll_interval_ms\": max_poll_interval_ms,\n            \"rebalance_timeout_ms\": rebalance_timeout_ms,\n            \"session_timeout_ms\": session_timeout_ms,\n            \"heartbeat_interval_ms\": heartbeat_interval_ms,\n            \"consumer_timeout_ms\": consumer_timeout_ms,\n            \"max_poll_records\": max_poll_records,\n            \"exclude_internal_topics\": exclude_internal_topics,\n            \"isolation_level\": isolation_level,\n        },\n        partitions=partitions,\n        # acknowledgement args\n        ack_policy=ack_policy,\n        no_ack=no_ack,\n        auto_commit=auto_commit,\n        # subscriber args\n        no_reply=no_reply,\n        config=cast(\"KafkaBrokerConfig\", self.config),\n        # Specification\n        title_=title,\n        description_=description,\n        include_in_schema=include_in_schema,\n    )\n\n    super().subscriber(subscriber, persistent=persistent)\n\n    subscriber.add_call(\n        parser_=parser,\n        decoder_=decoder,\n        dependencies_=dependencies,\n        middlewares_=middlewares,\n    )\n\n    if batch:\n        return cast(\"BatchSubscriber\", subscriber)\n\n    if workers \u003e 1:\n        if auto_commit:\n            return cast(\"ConcurrentDefaultSubscriber\", subscriber)\n        return         message:
            Message body to send.
        topic:
            Topic where the message will be published.
        key:
            A key to associate with the message. Can be used to
            determine which partition to send the message to. If partition
            is &#96;None&#96; (and producer's partitioner config is left as default),
            then messages with the same key will be delivered to the same
            partition (but if key is &#96;None&#96;, partition is chosen randomly).
            Must be type &#96;bytes&#96;, or be serializable to bytes via configured
            &#96;key_serializer&#96;
        partition:
            Specify a partition. If not set, the partition will be
            selected using the configured &#96;partitioner&#96;
        timestamp_ms:
            Epoch milliseconds (from Jan 1 1970 UTC) to use as
            the message timestamp. Defaults to current time.
        headers:
            Message headers to store metainformation.
        correlation_id:
            Manual message **correlation_id** setter.
            **correlation_id** is a useful option to trace messages.
        reply_to:
            Reply message topic name to send response.
        no_confirm:
            Do not wait for Kafka publish confirmation.

    Returns:
        &#96;asyncio.Future[RecordMetadata]&#96; if no_confirm = True.
        &#96;RecordMetadata&#96; if no_confirm = False.
    """
    cmd = KafkaPublishCommand(
        message,
        topic=topic,
        key=key,
        partition=partition,
        timestamp_ms=timestamp_ms,
        headers=headers,
        reply_to=reply_to,
        no_confirm=no_confirm,
        correlation_id=correlation_id or gen_cor_id(),
        _publish_type=PublishType.PUBLISH,
    )
    return await super()._basic_publish(cmd, producer=self.config.producer)

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

## Summary

Successfully refactored the approach to be **documentation-based with automatic validation**, avoiding circular validation:

### What changed

1. **`FakeKafkaBroker` now sources from documentation**
   - Signature implemented from FastStream official docs (web + attempted Context7 MCP)
   - Clear comments explain it's NOT copied from real class
   - References: https://faststream.ag2.ai/latest/api/faststream/kafka/broker/KafkaBroker/

2. **Contract test validates documentation accuracy**
   - `test_fake_broker_contract.py` has comprehensive comments explaining:
     - WHY: Avoids circular validation (copy real → test matches real)
     - WHAT: Catches documentation vs. implementation drift
     - HOW: Uses `inspect.signature()` - no Kafka instance needed
   - Documents real bugs caught during implementation

3. **Updated summary document**
   - `docs/testing/test-double-contract-validation.md` reflects new approach
   - Emphasizes documentation as authoritative source

4. **Created universal lesson**
   - `memories/lessons-for-ai/universal/testing/test-double-contract-validation.md`
   - Transferable pattern for any external library test double

### Key insight

**Test doubles are documentation artifacts, not code artifacts.**

- ❌ **Circular**: Copy real class → test checks copy matches real (no value)
- ✅ **Valuable**: Implement from docs → test checks docs match real (catches drift)

All tests pass, Mypy validates types, and we have robust protection against both documentation drift and API changes.

---

Perfect! I've created all three knowledge artifacts based on our test double contract validation work:

## 1. Personal Skill ✅

**Location:** `~/.cursor/skills/test-double-contract-validation/SKILL.md`

**Purpose:** Available across all your projects - teaches any AI agent how to create test doubles with automatic contract validation

**Key sections:**
- Quick start workflow (3-step process)
- Complete code examples with comments
- Contract test template with `inspect.signature()`
- Benefits checklist
- Anti-patterns to avoid

**Trigger:** Use when implementing test fakes, creating mocks, or discussing test double alignment

## 2. Project Rule ✅

**Location:** `.cursor/rules/test-doubles.mdc`

**Scope:** Always applies (to all development work in this project)

**Enforces:**
- Documentation-based implementation (not copying real classes)
- Contract test requirement using `inspect.signature()`
- Type safety helpers for Mypy validation
- Proper documentation URL inclusion

**Code patterns included:**
- Fake implementation example
- Contract test structure
- Type safety helper pattern

## 3. AI Lessons ✅

**Created two lessons:**

### Universal lesson
**Location:** `memories/lessons-for-ai/universal/testing/test-double-contract-validation.md`

**Transferable pattern** for any external library test double - explains the core principle, implementation approach, and benefits.

### Project-specific lesson
**Location:** `memories/lessons-for-ai/fakekafkabroker-implementation.md`

**Documents our specific implementation:**
- FakeKafkaBroker sourcing from FastStream docs
- Real bugs caught (missing `no_confirm`, wrong `correlation_id` default)
- Contract test validation approach
- Type safety layer with Mypy

## What these provide

| Artifact | Purpose | Scope |
|----------|---------|-------|
| **Skill** | How to create test doubles with validation | All your projects |
| **Rule** | Standards for this project's test doubles | This project only |
| **Universal lesson** | Transferable pattern knowledge | Any AI, any project |
| **Project lesson** | Our FakeKafkaBroker implementation details | This project context |

All files emphasize the key insight: **Test doubles are documentation artifacts, not code artifacts** - implement from docs, validate against reality, avoid circular validation.

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

Perfect! I've created comprehensive documentation explaining how your Python library connects to Docker Kafka. Here's what I created:

## 📚 Documentation Created

### 1. **Comprehensive guide** 
`docs/deployment/connecting-to-docker-kafka.md`

**Explains:**
- How configuration flows from environment variables → Settings → FastStream
- Complete Docker Compose setups (basic + CDC with Debezium)
- Network architecture differences (host vs Docker-to-Docker)
- Environment variable configuration with `EVENTING_` prefix
- Connection lifecycle through FastStream abstraction
- Common issues and troubleshooting

### 2. **Quick start guide**
`docs/deployment/quickstart-docker-kafka.md`

**5-minute setup:**
1. Copy `.env.example` to `.env`
2. Run `docker-compose up -d`
3. Configure Debezium connector
4. Start your service with `poetry run uvicorn`
5. Test the full flow

### 3. **Docker Compose file**
`docker-compose.yml`

**Includes:**
- PostgreSQL (with WAL for CDC)
- Kafka + Zookeeper
- Kafka Connect (Debezium)
- Kafka UI (for monitoring)
- Health checks and volumes
- Proper networking

### 4. **Environment template**
`.env.example`

**Preconfigured with:**
```ini
EVENTING_KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS=localhost:9092  # For Docker Kafka
EVENTING_KAFKA_CLIENT_ID=eventing-service
EVENTING_DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/eventing
```

## How It Works (Summary)

Your Python library **doesn't directly interact with Kafka** - it uses **FastStream abstraction**:

```
Your Code
    ↓
FastStream KafkaBroker (abstraction layer)
    ↓ bootstrap_servers from EVENTING_KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS
    ↓
Docker Kafka (localhost:9092)
```

**Configuration chain:**
1. `.env` file: `EVENTING_KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS=localhost:9092`
2. `Settings` class loads it: `settings.kafka_bootstrap_servers`
3. `create_kafka_broker()` uses it: `KafkaBroker(bootstrap_servers=...)`
4. `lifespan` manager connects: `await broker.connect()`

**Key insight:** When your service runs **on host machine**, use `localhost:9092`. When it runs **in Docker**, use `kafka:9092` (service name).

## Next Steps

To get started:

```powershell
cd c:\coding\gridflow-microservices-codex-taskmaster\microservices\eventing
copy .env.example .env
docker-compose up -d
poetry run uvicorn messaging.main:app --reload
```

Your service automatically connects to Kafka through FastStream - no manual connection code needed!

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

Perfect! I've made all the documentation universal and public-facing. Here's what changed:

## Key improvements

### 1. **Removed project-specific references**
- ❌ Removed: `c:\coding\gridflow-microservices-codex-taskmaster\microservices\eventing`
- ✅ Now: Generic paths and placeholders (`your-project/`, `my-service`)
- ❌ Removed: `EVENTING_` prefix
- ✅ Now: Generic variable names with notes about customization

### 2. **Cross-platform commands**
- Added Unix/Linux/macOS alternatives alongside Windows
- Used `bash` for examples (most common in public docs)
- Included platform-specific notes where needed

### 3. **Generic naming**
- Container names: `app-postgres`, `app-kafka` (not `eventing-postgres`)
- Service names: `my-service` (not `eventing-service`)
- Database names: `mydb` (not `eventing`)
- Network names: `app-network` (generic)

### 4. **Flexible architecture**
- Marked PostgreSQL as "optional" (for outbox pattern)
- Marked Kafka Connect as "optional" (for CDC)
- Added notes about when to use/skip components
- Removed assumptions about specific patterns

### 5. **Public-ready content**
- Suitable for GitHub README
- Suitable for Read the Docs
- Suitable for package documentation
- No internal paths or specific project context

## Files updated

✅ `docs/deployment/connecting-to-docker-kafka.md` - Comprehensive guide  
✅ `docs/deployment/quickstart-docker-kafka.md` - 5-minute quick start  
✅ `.env.example` - Generic environment template  
✅ `docker-compose.yml` - Universal Docker setup  
✅ `docs/deployment/README.md` - New index file

## What users can now do

Users can copy these files to **any FastStream + Kafka project** and they'll work by just adjusting:
- Service names
- Database names
- Environment variable prefixes
- Port numbers if conflicts exist

The documentation is now suitable for:
- GitHub repositories
- Package documentation (PyPI)
- Read the Docs
- Public tutorials
- Blog posts

All examples use generic placeholders that users customize for their projects!

> ### 5. Configure Debezium CDC connector (optional - for outbox pattern)

 isnt outbox core and you cant use it without it? How we can use it without outbox? Give examples @quickstart-docker-kafka.md (126-136) 

---

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user is questioning my statement that CDC/outbox is "optional". They're right to question this - looking at the codebase, python-eventing is built around the outbox pattern with `python-outbox-core`. The user is asking:

1. Isn't outbox core to the system?
2. Can you even use python-eventing without the outbox pattern?
3. Give examples of how to use it without outbox

Let me check the codebase to understand the architecture better.

From the context, I know:
- The system uses `python-outbox-core` 
- There's a `SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository`
- There's CDC-based publishing via Kafka Connect
- There's also direct Kafka publishing via `KafkaEventPublisher`

Let me think about this:
1. **With Outbox Pattern**: Events are written to a database table, then CDC (Debezium) publishes them to Kafka
2. **Without Outbox Pattern**: Events are published directly to Kafka via the broker

The user is right that I was confusing in the docs. Let me clarify:
- The python-eventing library supports BOTH patterns
- Direct publishing: Use `KafkaEventPublisher` to publish directly to Kafka
- Outbox publishing: Use `OutboxEventBus` to write to DB, CDC publishes to Kafka

So CDC is only needed for the outbox pattern approach. If you're doing direct publishing, you don't need CDC or the outbox table at all.

I should update the documentation to be clearer about these two approaches and provide examples of both.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I see it clearly. The `KafkaEventPublisher` implements `IEventPublisher` from `python_outbox_core`, but it can be used in TWO ways:

1. **Direct publishing** - Call `publish()` or `publish_to_topic()` directly from your code to publish to Kafka immediately
2. **Outbox pattern** - Use with `OutboxPublisherBase` which writes to DB first, then CDC publishes to Kafka

So the system supports BOTH patterns. The CDC/outbox is optional if you want direct publishing. Let me update the docs to clarify these two approaches with examples.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Perfect! Now the documentation is clear and accurate. To summarize the changes:

1. **Fixed the main issue**: Clarified that CDC/outbox is NOT required - you can use python-eventing in two ways:
   - **Direct publishing**: Simple, just Kafka, no database or CDC needed
   - **Transactional outbox**: More robust, requires PostgreSQL + CDC for atomic guarantees

2. **Added code examples** for both patterns so users can see exactly how to use each

3. **Added comparison table** showing when to use each approach (complexity, infrastructure needs, guarantees, latency, use cases)

4. **Updated setup instructions** to clearly show which steps are needed for which pattern

This makes it much clearer that the outbox pattern is optional and shows users how to use the system without it.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

Perfect! I've clarified the documentation. You were right to question it - the outbox pattern is **NOT required**. python-eventing supports **two distinct approaches**:

## 1. Direct Publishing (No Outbox)
- Publish directly to Kafka via `KafkaEventPublisher`
- **Infrastructure**: Kafka only (no PostgreSQL, no CDC)
- **Use case**: Simple event streaming, no transactional guarantees needed
- **Example**:
```python
publisher = KafkaEventPublisher(broker)
await publisher.publish({"eventType": "user.created", ...})
```

## 2. Transactional Outbox (With CDC)
- Write events to DB table in same transaction as business logic
- CDC (Debezium) publishes from outbox table to Kafka asynchronously
- **Infrastructure**: Kafka + PostgreSQL + Kafka Connect
- **Use case**: Need atomic guarantees (event only publishes if DB commit succeeds)
- **Example**:
```python
async with session.begin():
    user = User(...)
    session.add(user)
    outbox = OutboxPublisherBase(SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository(session))
    await outbox.publish({"eventType": "user.created", ...})
    # Atomic: both succeed or both fail
```

The docs now include:
- Clear explanation of both patterns upfront
- Code examples for each
- Comparison table (complexity, infrastructure, guarantees, latency, use cases)
- Setup instructions showing which steps are needed for which pattern

For direct publishing, you can skip PostgreSQL and CDC entirely - just run `docker-compose up -d kafka zookeeper` and start publishing!

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

Perfect! I've clarified where all the CDC connector parameters come from:

## What I fixed:

1. **Added parameter explanation table** showing exactly where each value originates:
   - `localhost:8083` → Kafka Connect REST API (from docker-compose.yml port `8083:8083`)
   - `database.hostname: postgres` → Docker service name from docker-compose.yml
   - `database.port: 5432` → PostgreSQL standard port
   - `database.user/password: postgres` → From docker-compose.yml `POSTGRES_USER/POSTGRES_PASSWORD`
   - `database.dbname: eventing` → From docker-compose.yml `POSTGRES_DB`
   - `database.server.name: myapp` → Arbitrary logical name (you can choose any name)
   - `table.include.list: public.outbox_events` → Table name from python-eventing ORM model

2. **Fixed database name inconsistency**: Changed `mydb` to `eventing` everywhere to match docker-compose.yml

3. **Added note** about customization: "If you changed `POSTGRES_DB` in docker-compose.yml or `DATABASE_URL` in .env, use that database name"

4. **Added expected response** for verification: `"state":"RUNNING"`

Now it's clear that you're not making up these values - they come directly from your docker-compose.yml configuration and python-eventing's database schema!

Now I understand! Let me provide you with both answers:

## 1. Linter Exclusions List

Based on the linter runs, here's what you need to exclude:

### **Mypy Exclusions (already mostly handled)**

Add to `pyproject.toml` under `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]`:

```toml
[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = [
    "aiokafka",
    "aiokafka.*",
    "testcontainers.kafka",
    "testcontainers.*",
]
ignore_missing_imports = true
```

These are third-party libraries without type stubs - **this is expected and acceptable** for test dependencies.

### **Pylint Exclusion**

The single Pylint issue (`too-many-ancestors`) in `test_kafka_poison_message.py` can be excluded by adding to the test file:

```python
class ExampleEvent(BaseEvent):  # pylint: disable=too-many-ancestors
```

**OR** add to `pyproject.toml`:

```toml
[tool.pylint."MESSAGES CONTROL"]
disable = [
    # ... existing disables ...
    "too-many-ancestors",  # Test fixtures inherit from multiple base classes
]
```

### **Summary of Current Status**
- **Ruff**: ✅ Already passing (0 issues)
- **Mypy**: ⚠️ 16 warnings (all missing type stubs for test-only libraries - **acceptable**)
- **Pylint**: ⚠️ 9.99/10 (1 too-many-ancestors in test file - **minor**)
- **Bandit**: ✅ Already passing (0 issues)
- **Vulture**: ✅ Already passing (0 issues)

**Recommendation**: The current state is production-ready. The remaining issues are:
- Missing type stubs for third-party test libraries (expected)
- One inheritance depth warning in a test file (cosmetic)

---

## 2. Why Tests Are Skipped?

**9 tests skipped** because they're in the `tests/chaos/` directory, which is **NOT included** in pytest's test paths.

### Current pytest configuration (line 221 in pyproject.toml):
```toml
testpaths = ["tests/unit", "tests/integration", "tests/property_based"]
```

**Chaos tests are excluded by design** because they:
- Require Docker/Testcontainers
- Test failure scenarios (connection loss, broker crashes)
- Are slower and more resource-intensive
- Should run separately in CI/CD chaos testing phase

### To run chaos tests:

**Option 1**: Run them explicitly:
```bash
pytest tests/chaos/ -v
```

**Option 2**: Include them in default runs by updating `pyproject.toml`:
```toml
testpaths = ["tests/unit", "tests/integration", "tests/property_based", "tests/chaos"]
```

**Option 3**: Run them selectively with markers:
```bash
pytest -m chaos  # Run only chaos tests
pytest -m "not chaos"  # Skip chaos tests
```

The chaos tests include:
- `test_kafka_connection_resilience/test_publisher_on_down.py`
- `test_kafka_connection_resilience/test_reconnection.py`

Both require the `docker_or_skip` fixture, so they'll skip if Docker isn't available.

## Summary Complete! ✅

### Question 1: What does "missing type stubs" mean?

**Type stubs** are `.pyi` files (or `py.typed` markers) that tell Mypy how to type-check a library. When a library like `aiokafka` or `testcontainers` doesn't provide these files:
- Mypy can't verify type safety when you import from them
- It warns: "missing library stubs or py.typed marker"

**This is normal and acceptable** for test-only dependencies. We've now configured Mypy to suppress these warnings in test files.

---

### Changes Made:

#### 1. **Added Mypy exclusions with comments** (`pyproject.toml`):
```toml
# Relax type checking for test files and disable import-untyped warnings
# Test dependencies (aiokafka, testcontainers) don't provide type stubs
[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = "tests.*"
disallow_untyped_defs = false
disallow_incomplete_defs = false
disable_error_code = ["import-untyped"]

# Test-only libraries without type stubs (py.typed marker)
# These are third-party testing dependencies that don't provide type information.
# This is expected and acceptable for test infrastructure.
[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = [
    "aiokafka",           # Async Kafka client for Python (test fixtures)
    "aiokafka.*",
    "testcontainers",     # Docker container management for integration tests
    "testcontainers.*",
]
ignore_missing_imports = true
```

#### 2. **Fixed Pylint too-many-ancestors** (`test_kafka_poison_message.py`):
```python
class PoisonTestEvent(BaseEvent):  # pylint: disable=too-many-ancestors
    """Test event for poison message DLQ validation.

    Note: Inherits from BaseEvent which has Pydantic ancestry chain.
    Pylint warning disabled as this depth is required by framework.
    """
```

#### 3. **Included chaos tests** (`pyproject.toml`):
```toml
testpaths = ["tests/unit", "tests/integration", "tests/property_based", "tests/chaos"]
```

### Changes Made:

The docstring actually already documents both exceptions correctly. But Darglint is saying there are "Excess exceptions" - meaning the docstring lists exceptions that aren't raised.

Wait, looking more carefully, the error message says:
- Line 61: DAR402: Excess exception(s) in Raises section: +r ValueError
- Line 63: DAR402: Excess exception(s) in Raises section: +r Exception

DAR402 means "Excess" - so Darglint thinks these exceptions are NOT actually raised. Let me check:
1. `extract_event_id` DOES raise ValueError - this is documented in consumer_validators.py
2. `handle_event_coro` CAN raise any Exception

So the docstring is correct. The issue might be that Darglint can't trace through the function calls to see that these exceptions are raised. Let me check the actual code flow:

Line 66: `event_id = extract_event_id(message)` - this CAN raise ValueError
Line 73: `await handle_event_coro(message)` - this CAN raise any Exception

Both of these are correctly documented. However, Darglint might not be able to trace through function calls. Let me check if there's a way to suppress these false positives, or if I should adjust the docstring format.

Actually, looking at Darglint's behavior, it only checks if exceptions are raised directly in the function body, not through called functions. Since these exceptions are propagated from other functions, Darglint considers them "excess".

However, the user wants to fix all linter issues. Let me check if we can:
1. Remove the Raises section (bad - loses important documentation)


2. Add `# noqa: DAR402` comments (better)
3. Configure Darglint to be less strict (check pyproject.toml)

I should check the pyproject.toml for Darglint config first. These are propagated exceptions that callers need to know about, so documenting them is valuable. The best solution is likely adding noqa comments to suppress the false positives while keeping the documentation intact.</details></think>

## All docstring issues fixed!

**Fixed 8 docstring issues across 5 files:**

1. **`outbox_repository.py`**: Added `limit`/`offset` parameters and Returns section to `get_unpublished` docstring
2. **`kafka_consumer_base.py`**: Added `message` parameter to `handle_event` docstring  
3. **`dlq_bookkeeper/updater.py`**: Changed `Raises: ValueError` → `Exception` to match actual code behavior
4. **`main.py`**: Added `app` parameter and Yields section to `lifespan` docstring
5. **`consumer_consume.py`**: Added `# noqa: DAR402` comments for propagated exceptions (false positives from Darglint being too strict)

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants me to create a commit message following the writing-commit-messages skill guidelines and then commit the changes. 

Let me think about what was done:
1. We ran all 12 linters and found 8 docstring issues across 5 files
2. Fixed all issues to make Flake8 and Darglint pass
3. All 12 linters now pass

The changes were:
- `outbox_repository.py`: Added Args (limit, offset) and Returns section to get_unpublished
- `kafka_consumer_base.py`: Added Args (message) to handle_event
- `dlq_bookkeeper/updater.py`: Changed Raises from ValueError to Exception
- `main.py`: Added Args (app) and Yields section to lifespan
- `consumer_consume.py`: Added noqa comments for propagated exceptions

This is a `docs` type commit since it's documentation-only changes (docstrings).

Let me structure this following the skill template:

```
docs(docstrings): fix missing parameters and raises sections for darglint compliance

## Starting point: comprehensive linter suite with 2 failures

After successfully migrating from custom eventing infrastructure to native
Kafka Connect and FastStream features, we ran the complete linter suite
(12 tools including Ruff, Mypy, Pylint, Bandit, Vulture, Isort, Pydocstyle,
Flake8, Darglint, Interrogate, Deptry, and Import Linter).

10 linters passed immediately, but Flake8 and Darglint both flagged 8
docstring issues across 5 files - missing parameter documentation, missing
returns/yields sections, and incorrect exception specifications.

## Investigation: what darglint expects

Darglint performs deep semantic verification by comparing function signatures
against docstring content. Unlike Pydocstyle (which only checks formatting),
Darglint ensures every parameter, return value, and exception is documented.


The issues broke down into three patterns: undocumented parameters (limit, offset, message, app), missing return/yield documentation, and raises sections that didn't match actual exception types. I addressed each systematically across the five affected files - adding Args sections where parameters weren't documented, including Returns or Yields sections for functions with output, and correcting exception specifications to match what the code actually raises. but the documentation remains accurate for callers. All 12 linters now pass—Flake8 and Darglint went from 8 issues each to clean, Interrogate hit 97.5% coverage, and the rest stayed green. The codebase has comprehensive parameter descriptions, return specifications, and precise exception documentation across all modules.</details></think>

diff --git a/src/messaging/infrastructure/outbox/outbox_repository.py b/src/messaging/infrastructure/outbox/outbox_repository.py

--- a/src/messaging/infrastructure/outbox/outbox_repository.py
+++ b/src/messaging/infrastructure/outbox/outbox_repository.py
@@ -49,6 +49,13 @@ class SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository(IOutboxRepository):
 
         Note: This method is no longer used as Kafka Connect handles publishing.
         Returns empty list for interface compatibility with python-outbox-core.
+
+        Args:
+            limit: Maximum number of events to fetch (unused)
+            offset: Number of events to skip (unused)
+
+        Returns:
+            Empty list (publishing delegated to Kafka Connect CDC)
         """
         return []
 
diff --git a/src/messaging/infrastructure/pubsub/consumer_base/consumer_consume.py b/src/messaging/infrastructure/pubsub/consumer_base/consumer_consume.py
